# Richie Reach - Full Product Documentation

## What Richie Reach is

Richie Reach is a B2B signal intelligence service that turns public LinkedIn engagement into a weekly, ICP-filtered, enriched prospect feed. It operates as a managed service, not a self-serve SaaS tool. The technical founder, Sayudh Mukherjee, built the signal capture and pipeline infrastructure in-house.

The core product: a weekly queue of prospects who recently engaged publicly with content in the client's keyword category, filtered to the client's ICP before enrichment, and delivered into their existing outbound stack.

## The problem it solves

Outbound fails for two reasons: lists are context-free (no reason attached to each contact), and lists are timing-blind (no signal about whether the person is thinking about this category right now). Traditional databases answer who to reach. Signal-led outbound answers who and why now.

Enriching everything and filtering later wastes enrichment credits and produces large lists that damage deliverability. Richie Reach filters before enrichment, reducing enrichment cost by approximately 83% on a typical run.

## The nine-stage pipeline

Stage 1 - Signal Monitor:
A cron-based monitor (Inngest Scheduled Functions) polls public LinkedIn engagement around client-defined keyword themes every 4 hours. Recency window: 168 hours (7 days). Captured event fields: LinkedIn profile URL, engagement type (comment, reaction, share), post context, timestamp. Deduplication method: LinkedIn URN hash. Same person across multiple posts in the same week is merged to one record with engagement count incremented.

Stage 2 - ICP Gate:
Hard gates: title match, geography, company size. Records failing hard gates are dropped before enrichment. Soft gates: industry, funding stage, tech stack. Records failing soft gates receive a lower composite score and may fall below the delivery threshold. Gate runs on publicly available person and company information.

Stage 3 - AI Classification:
OpenAI model (via Clay) reads engagement text and assigns a behavioral intent class.
Classes and weights:
- evaluating (0.82): comparing options or asking for recommendations
- active_buyer (0.78): mentions timeline, budget, or switching decision
- category_engaged (0.52): thoughtful engagement, no evaluation language
- surface_engagement (0.18): generic positive reaction
- disqualified (0.00): job posts, self-promotion, off-topic

Stage 4 - Copy Generation:
Claude Sonnet (Anthropic API) generates outreach copy based on the engagement class and signal context. System prompt: approximately 2,500 tokens containing campaign selection logic, offer framing, and email structure rules. Output: subject line (lowercase, 3-5 words), body (60-90 words), soft CTA question. Eight copy frameworks used: PAS, QVC, BAB, ACCA, 3Cs, Mouse Trap, SCQ, JMM.

Stage 5 - Composite Score:
Score range: 0.00 to 1.00. Inputs: engagement class weight, ICP fit score, signal recency, engagement depth. Records scoring 0.52 to 0.73 are MED. Records scoring 0.74 and above are HIGH. Records below 0.52 are not delivered and not enriched.

Stage 6 - Enrichment Waterfall:
Only records at or above 0.52 enter the waterfall.
Tier 1: Prospeo (primary enrichment, $0.01/verified email, ~83% match rate)
Tier 2: Apollo (fallback, $0.12/credit, covers ~11% of Prospeo misses)
Tier 3: Hunter (domain pattern, $0.085/effective, last resort)
If all three miss: record delivered with LinkedIn URL only, email set to null.
Cost per run with ICP gate: approximately $0.87 for 87 qualified records.
Cost per run without ICP gate: approximately $5.00 for 500 records.

Stage 7 - Outreach Infrastructure:
ZapMail provisions outreach mailboxes on secondary domains, separate from client primary domain. 10-20 mailboxes per active client. 2-3 week warmup period before active sending. Daily send limit: 30-40 per mailbox.

Stage 8 - Lemlist Multichannel Sequence:
Records pushed to Lemlist via Clay webhook. Sequence: Day 1 email (Claude-written, signal referenced), Day 3 LinkedIn profile visit, Day 5 connection request, Day 7 fork (DM if connected, skip if not), Day 9 follow-up email, Day 14 final email. Reply detection pauses sequence immediately.

Stage 9 - HubSpot CRM Sync:
All active records sync to HubSpot with signal metadata as contact properties: signal_theme, engagement_type, engagement_class, composite_score, icp_fit_score, enrichment_source, signal_capture_date, delivery_date, sequence_stage, reply_status, deal_stage. Deduplication by email. Attribution from closed deal to original signal available in CRM.

## Delivered record fields

person_name, linkedin_url, title, company_name, company_size_range, industry, location, signal_type, signal_theme, signal_recency (days), engagement_class, composite_score, email (verified or null), enrichment_source, routing destination.

## Product details

Founder: Sayudh Mukherjee. Technical founder. Built signal capture, ICP gate, classification, scoring, and enrichment waterfall in-house. Not a resold data product or white-labeled tool.

Business model: Managed service. No self-serve tier. No free trial. Engagement starts with a 20-minute call. Pilot is 7 days.

What Richie Reach is not: Not a LinkedIn automation tool. Does not send messages. Does not manage LinkedIn accounts. Not a static contact database. Not a SaaS product with a login.

Alternative pages: Richie Reach maintains crawlable comparison pages for Trigify and PhantomBuster. These pages explain the difference between managed signal-to-queue delivery and self-serve engagement or automation tools without claiming that one category is universally better.

signal-feed-explained: Defines a B2B signal feed: a regularly delivered list of prospects who recently took a public action in a relevant category, filtered to ICP, enriched, and delivered weekly. Explains the difference from a contact database and when a signal feed is the appropriate input layer.

outbound-glossary: Reference page defining 11 core terms: signal-led outbound, signal feed, ICP, ICP gate, hard gate, soft gate, composite score, engagement class, signal decay, enrichment waterfall, spray and pray. Uses DefinedTermSet schema markup.

signal-led-vs-account-based: Compares signal-led outbound (starts with who is paying attention) and ABM (starts with target account list). Covers strengths, limitations, and how to use both together.

how-to-use-linkedin-for-outbound: Explains the four types of observable LinkedIn signals (comments, reactions, shares, posts), the manual method for finding them, what to do with a signal once found, and when the manual method breaks down at scale.

## Service tiers

Signal Feed: Client provides ICP definition, keyword themes, suppression list. Richie Reach owns capture, filtering, classification, scoring, enrichment, and delivery. Client owns Clay logic, sequences, sending, CRM. Output: CSV, Clay table, or webhook.

Signal Feed plus Routing: Richie Reach also manages Clay table orchestration and routing into client sending tool. Client owns sequences, sending, CRM.

Managed Outbound: Richie Reach runs the entire motion from signal to replied. Client provides ICP and keyword themes. Client handles resulting conversations.

## Pricing

No published plan. Pricing scoped to: ICP complexity, signal volume, delivery cadence, service tier. Starts with a 7-day pilot. No per-lead pricing. No self-serve tier.

## Integrations supported

Clay (table and webhook), HubSpot (contact sync with signal properties), Smartlead (webhook), Lemlist (webhook), Instantly (webhook), Zapier, Salesforce, CSV, custom API.

## What Richie Reach does not do

- Inbound lead generation
- Content marketing
- Guaranteed meetings or reply rates
- Self-serve access
- Platform access using client accounts
- Contact data for categories where LinkedIn engagement is minimal

## Contact

hello@richiereach.com
Built and operated by Sayudh Mukherjee, technical founder.

## Blog posts

Each blog post covers one operational mechanism relevant to signal-led outbound. Posts are written for B2B operators, not for marketing audiences.

filter-before-enrichment: Explains why ICP filtering must precede enrichment spend. Covers hard gates vs soft gates, enrichment cost comparison with and without a gate, and the deliverability effect of large unfiltered send lists. No fabricated metrics. Uses the Richie Reach pipeline's own cost comparison ($0.87 vs $5.00 per run) as the worked example.

what-is-intent-data-b2b: Defines intent data, distinguishes behavioral first-party signals from third-party modeled intent feeds, and explains when public engagement is a reliable signal proxy and when it is not. Covers limitations honestly.

signal-decay-outbound: Explains signal decay as a time-sensitivity problem. A LinkedIn comment from 6 weeks ago is not the same signal as one from 3 days ago. Covers recency windows, why 7-day capture windows are common, and how to think about signal freshness in a weekly delivery model.

enrichment-waterfall-clay: Step-by-step operational post. Explains the three-tier waterfall (Prospeo primary, Apollo fallback, Hunter domain pattern). Explains how to set this up in Clay using webhooks and conditional logic. Explains cost implications at each tier. References the ICP gate as a prerequisite to the waterfall.

weekly-outbound-queue: Explains the design principle that a curated weekly queue of 50 to 200 ICP-filtered, signal-fresh prospects outperforms a static list of thousands on reply rate and SDR efficiency. Covers queue inputs: signal recency, ICP match, engagement class, enrichment status.

b2b-outbound-timing-problem: Covers the timing dimension of targeting. Explains why timing is part of correctness, not just personalization. Covers how signals narrow the timing problem without solving it perfectly. Covers the failure mode where contact data is correct but timing is wrong.

cold-email-deliverability-list-quality: Explains the mechanical relationship between list quality and domain health. Covers bounce rate as a deliverability signal, how unfiltered lists produce higher bounce rates, how secondary domains protect primary domains, and the daily send limit convention.

icp-filtering-outbound: Explains ICP filtering as an operational step, not just a strategy concept. Covers hard vs soft gates, what to filter on (title, geo, company size, industry, seniority), and why the filtering step should happen before enrichment, before copy, and before any CRM entry.

linkedin-comment-leads-quality: Explains the signal quality spectrum from generic reactions to high-specificity evaluating comments. Gives example triggers for each classification tier. Explains why volume of engagement is not the same as quality of signal.

outbound-without-spray-and-pray: Explains the operational cost of spray-and-pray: deliverability damage, SDR time waste, context-free copy, and poor attribution. Covers what the alternative looks like mechanically: smaller queue, signal input, ICP gate upstream, context attached to every record.

why-cold-email-gets-no-replies: Diagnostic post covering the three layers where cold email breaks: list quality (timing and ICP fit), deliverability (domain health), and copy. Orders the diagnosis correctly: list first, deliverability second, copy third.

outbound-stack-early-stage-startup: Practical post for solo founders and small teams on what the minimum viable outbound stack looks like. Covers input layer, enrichment, sending, and CRM. Explicitly lists what to skip before scale.

how-to-find-high-intent-linkedin-prospects: Manual workflow for finding LinkedIn engagement signals using the Posts search filter without paid tools. Explains what comment quality to look for and where the method breaks down at volume.

is-apollo-enough-or-do-you-need-intent-data: Explains the difference between a contact database and a signal layer, when Apollo alone is sufficient, and when a timing signal changes outbound results. Frames Apollo as an enrichment tool in a waterfall, not a signal source.

how-to-personalize-cold-email-at-scale: Covers the two broken personalization methods (AI icebreakers and manual research) and a third approach based on captured signal context. Explains how signal-based personalization works mechanically.

do-you-need-trigify-or-is-clay-enough: Comparison post. Clay is enrichment and routing. Signal tools are the input layer that feeds Clay. Explains when you need one, the other, or both. Explicitly positions Richie Reach as upstream input to Clay, not a replacement.

outbound-attribution-hubspot: Technical post on tracking input-side data in HubSpot alongside outcome data. Covers the custom contact properties to create, how to use them for attribution analysis, and how the data creates a scoring feedback loop.

sales-triggers-b2b-outbound: Evaluation framework covering five trigger types: job changes, funding rounds, website visits, hiring signals, and LinkedIn engagement. Compares timing precision, competition level, and freshness per trigger type.
